icd_category: K25.1 — Acute gastric ulcer with perforation
one_liner: A 55-year-old male with chronic NSAID use presents with sudden-onset severe epigastric pain, rigid abdomen, and free air on upright chest X-ray.

stage2.hpi_extension: Patient describes a sudden onset of severe, sharp epigastric pain that began approximately 4 hours ago while he was sitting at his desk. He describes the pain as 'like being stabbed in the stomach' and rates it 10/10. The pain initially was localized to the epigastrium but has now spread to involve the entire abdomen. He is lying completely still because any movement worsens the pain. He has had one episode of dry heaving but no vomiting. He has had intermittent epigastric burning and indigestion for the past 2 months but did not seek treatment. He takes ibuprofen 800 mg three times daily for chronic back pain and has been doing so for over a year. He also smokes and drinks daily. He denies fever, hematemesis, melena, or recent weight loss.
stage2.pmh: Chronic low back pain, peptic ulcer disease (diagnosed 10 years ago, did not complete H. pylori treatment), hypertension
stage2.medications: Ibuprofen 800 mg TID, lisinopril 20 mg daily
stage2.family_history: Father had gastric cancer at age 72.
stage2.social_history: Smokes 1 pack/day for 30 years. Drinks 3-4 beers daily. Works as a truck driver. Divorced, lives alone.
stage2.physical_exam: General: **Ill-appearing male lying completely motionless on stretcher**; **Diaphoretic**; **Knees drawn up**; **Grimaces with any movement**. HEENT: **Dry mucous membranes**. Neck: **Supple**. Cardiovascular: **Tachycardic, regular rhythm**; **S1/S2 present**. Pulmonary: **Shallow respirations**; **Decreased breath sounds at both bases**; No crackles. Abdomen: **RIGID ('board-like') diffusely**; **Severe tenderness throughout with maximum pain in the epigastrium**; **Involuntary guarding**; **Positive rebound tenderness**; **Absent bowel sounds**; No distension; **Periumbilical tenderness to percussion**. Rectal: **Guaiac negative**. Extremities: **Cool, pale**; **Capillary refill 3 seconds**. Skin: **Diaphoretic, pale**.

lab_results.clinical_significance: Leukocytosis from peritonitis and systemic inflammatory response
Mildly elevated; can occur with perforated ulcer (non-pancreatic source)
Active H. pylori infection contributing to ulcer disease

imaging_results.result: Free air (pneumoperitoneum) under both hemidiaphragms, more prominent on the right. No pulmonary infiltrates.
imaging_results.clinical_significance: Free air under the diaphragm is the hallmark of perforated hollow viscus — confirms perforation

imaging_results.result: Pneumoperitoneum with extraluminal air adjacent to the anterior wall of the gastric antrum. Fluid in the anterior peritoneal space. Thickened antral wall at the site of perforation. No abscess. No other organ injury.
imaging_results.clinical_significance: Localizes the perforation to the gastric antrum and characterizes the extent of contamination

low_yield_tests: Upper GI series with oral contrast (delays surgery)

stage5.final_clue: Upright chest X-ray shows free air under the diaphragm. CT localizes the perforation to the gastric antrum. The patient has a rigid abdomen with peritonitis, chronic NSAID use, and a history of peptic ulcer disease with untreated H. pylori. This is a this condition requiring emergent surgical repair.

debrief.classic_presentation: Sudden onset of severe epigastric pain ('thunderclap abdomen') that rapidly becomes diffuse, with rigid/board-like abdomen, absent bowel sounds, and peritonitis. Free air on upright CXR (present in 75-80% of cases). Risk factors: NSAID use (strongest modifiable risk factor), H. pylori infection, smoking, alcohol, prior ulcer history, and corticosteroid use.
debrief.pathophysiology: Peptic ulcers form when gastric acid and pepsin overwhelm mucosal defense mechanisms. NSAIDs inhibit COX-1-mediated prostaglandin synthesis, reducing mucosal blood flow, bicarbonate secretion, and mucus production. H. pylori infection damages the epithelium and promotes acid secretion. When an ulcer erodes through all layers of the gastric or duodenal wall (transmural penetration), it perforates, releasing gastric contents (acid, bile, bacteria, food) into the peritoneal cavity. This causes chemical peritonitis initially, followed by bacterial peritonitis within hours. The anterior wall of the duodenal bulb and the gastric antrum are the most common perforation sites.
debrief.treatment_overview: Emergent surgical repair: Graham patch (omental patch repair) via laparoscopic or open approach is the standard of care. Pre-operative resuscitation: IV crystalloid bolus, broad-spectrum antibiotics (piperacillin-tazobactam or cephalosporin + metronidazole), NPO, NGT decompression, Foley catheter, pain management. Peritoneal lavage during surgery. Post-operative: IV PPI (pantoprazole 80 mg bolus then 8 mg/hr infusion), H. pylori eradication (triple therapy), and definitive discontinuation of NSAIDs. Follow-up endoscopy in 6-8 weeks to confirm ulcer healing and rule out malignancy (for gastric ulcers).
high_yield_pearls: Free air on upright CXR is present in only 75-80% of perforations — its absence does NOT exclude the diagnosis.
Board-like rigidity with absent bowel sounds in the setting of sudden epigastric pain is the classic presentation of perforation.
NSAIDs + H. pylori together have a synergistic effect on ulcer risk (>60x increased risk).
Posterior duodenal ulcers tend to BLEED (proximity to gastroduodenal artery); anterior ulcers tend to PERFORATE (into free peritoneal cavity).
All gastric ulcers should be biopsied at follow-up endoscopy to exclude malignancy; duodenal ulcers rarely undergo malignant transformation.
sources: Schwartz's Principles of Surgery, 11th Edition, Ch. 26
